coredata.dc:title: Dual-QDs ratios fluorescent probe for sensitive and stable detection of insulin
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Guanggui
Sun, Zhong
Wu, Yuntang
Sai, Na
coredata.dc:description: In this work, immune modified graphene quantum dot (GQD) and semiconductor quantum dot (SQD) with blue and red emission respectively were synthesized to assemble a dual-QDs ratios fluorescent probe, which could be efficient used for insulin determination. There may be the dynamic equilibrium of förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and aggregation-induced emission (AIE) in the internal of the probe, thus emitted special dual fluorescent lights. However, this sate of probe was cleaved upon exposure to target insulin, resulting in changing of the dual fluorescent lights. The resulting ratios response can be correlated quantitatively to the concentration of insulin, and was found to have a detection limit (as low as 0.045 ng mL−1) and rapid response time (as short as 5 min). It has been preliminarily used for ratiometric sensing of insulin in biological samples and exhibited consistency of the insulin detected results and higher stability compared with conventional ELISA. Therefore, this sensitive, rapid and stable detection system has great potential for next generation of the bioassay platform for clinical diagnosis and other applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-QDs ratios fluorescent probe
Förster resonance energy transfer
Aggregation-induced emission
Detection
Insulin

Insulin detection of the dual-QDs ratios fluorescent probe
Dual fluorescent characteristics of the probe
The dual-QDs ratios fluorescent probe for insulin detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the funding of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant 81302430 ) and Natural Science Foundation of Tianjin (Grant 18JCQNJC10800 ), and Chinese Nutrition Society-Bright Moon Seaweed Nutrition and Health Research Fund (Grant CNS-BMSG2020A60).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The sensing mechanism of the dual-QDs ratios fluorescent probe.
TEM images of Ab-GQD (a), Ag-SQD (b) and the mixture of Ab-GQD and Ag-SQD (c). The photographs of Ab-GQD and Ag-SQD solution under UV lamp irradiation (Insets of Fig. 2a and Fig. 2b).
The fluorescent excitation-emission mappings of Ab-GQD at different concentrations under excitation of different wavelengths (350 nm − 400 nm): (a) C0; (b) C0/4; (c) C0/8.

(a) The spectral overlap between the emission spectrum of Ab-GQD and the excitation spectrum of Ag-SQD; (b) Dual fluorescent characteristics of the probe; (c) Fluorescence quenching of Ab-GQD in the absence (black line) and presence (other colors) of concentration of Ag-SQD.

(a) Dual fluorescence changes of the probe in the present of insulin with different concentration of insulin; (b) Effect of insulin with different concentration on Ab-GQD and Ag-SQD alone; (c) Ratio fluorescent response and the regression curve of the probe for insulin with different concentration.

(a) Ratio fluorescent response of the probe from 1 min to 40 min; (b) Ratio fluorescent response of the probe for interfering ions (Fe2+, Cu2+, Na+ and NH4+) and analogs of insulin (CRP, urea and BSA); (c) Ratio fluorescent response of the probe for the salt with different concentration.
Comparison between the dual-QDs ratios fluorescent probe and traditional ELISA.

head.ce:title: Dual-QDs ratios fluorescent probe for sensitive and stable detection of insulin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nutrition and Food Hygiene, School of Public Health, Tianjin Medical University, Tianjin 300070, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Nutrition and Food Hygiene

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Nutrition and Food Hygiene, School of Public Health, Tianjin Medical University, Tianjin 300070, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, immune modified graphene quantum dot (GQD) and semiconductor quantum dot (SQD) with blue and red emission respectively were synthesized to assemble a dual-QDs ratios fluorescent probe, which could be efficient used for insulin determination. There may be the dynamic equilibrium of förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and aggregation-induced emission (AIE) in the internal of the probe, thus emitted special dual fluorescent lights. However, this sate of probe was cleaved upon exposure to target insulin, resulting in changing of the dual fluorescent lights. The resulting ratios response can be correlated quantitatively to the concentration of insulin, and was found to have a detection limit (as low as 0.045 ng mL−1) and rapid response time (as short as 5 min). It has been preliminarily used for ratiometric sensing of insulin in biological samples and exhibited consistency of the insulin detected results and higher stability compared with conventional ELISA. Therefore, this sensitive, rapid and stable detection system has great potential for next generation of the bioassay platform for clinical diagnosis and other applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent methods were time-saving, low-cost and easy to miniaturize for a large variety of applications, such as environmental monitoring, food safety and clinical diagnosis [1–2]. The traditional fluorescent methods based on fluorescent organic dyes generally suffered from photobleaching and serious background interference. In addition, the fluorescent organic dyes often exhibited a wide emission band and a narrow excitation wavelength and involved complex syntheses and purification processes [3–5]. The emerging of quantum dots (QDs), such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and semiconductor quantum dots (SQDs), admirably solved the above problems due to its narrow emission, long-term photostability and simple preparation [6–9]. In addition, the use of these QDs can enhance the advanced methods the capability of analytical devices for the sensitive determination of trivial quantity of target molecules and also improve the functional and structural stability [10–11].

Insulin is a polypeptide hormone from mammalian pancreatic cells that plays an important role in glucose metabolism towards maintaining normal blood glucose concentrations [12–13]. The level of insulin has been proved to be an important critical indicator of the endocrinal function of beta cells and serves as a valuable basis for the diagnosis of diabetes mellitus, insulinoma, insulin resistant syndrome, etc [14]. In particular, diabetes is one of the most commonly occurring chronic diseases, and it affects over 340 million people worldwide. Obesity, sedentary lifestyles, western dietary habits and aging will continue to drive a dramatic growth of diabetes for decades. A rapid and reliable laboratory test for the detection of insulin levels in serum can be very important to diagnose diabetes in its early stages [15].

The traditional methods for insulin detection involved enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [16–17], ratioimmunoassay [18], mass spectrometry [19] and chromatography techniques [20]. In addition, there have been various fluorescent methods developed based on the association of antibody (Ab), antigen (Ag) or DNA duplex bringing together fluorophores to generate förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [21–22], plasmon-enhanced fluorescent aggregation-induced emission (AIE) probe [23], gold nanoclusters labeled antibody bead-based fluorescence sandwich immunoassay [24] and so on [25–26]. However, most of the reported detection methods for insulin are single-emission turn-on sensors that can be difficult to quantify in a complex biological environment. A ratio responsive (i.e. light-changing and thus self-calibrating) assay would address this problem.

In this work, we constructed the dual-QDs ratios fluorescent probe for the detection of insulin. As shown in Fig. 1 , GQD and SQD with blue and red emissions respectively were self-assembled to form the probe after immune functionalization. In the probe, the GQD may have AIE effect and transfer the energy to SQD by FRET, thus exhibiting the special dual fluorescent features. This state can be broken by the exposure to target insulin, accordingly creating ratio fluorescent changes with an increase of insulin concentration. Making use of this probe, the insulin amount in the biological serum was primary detected and the detection performances were compared with conventional ELISA. To the best of our knowledge, there have been few reports on the ratio fluorescent probe for the detection of insulin or other relevant diagnostic indicators.

All chemical regents in this experiment were of analytical grade. 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), bovine serum albumin (BSA), insulin, C-reactive protein (CRP) and urea were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. The monoclonal against insulin Ab was obtained by Shanghai Yaji Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). NaHTe, CdCl2, NaBH4 and other common solvents and salts were supplied by Tianjin Regent Corp. (Tianjin, China). Clinical routine ELISA testing kit was purchased by Boo Sai Biotechnology Corp. (Tianjin, China). Water was purified by a Millipore M−Q purification system (>18 Mcm). Phosphate buffer saline (PBS) was employed as a wash buffer and was sterilized and prepared fresh daily.

The 96 wells costar opaque black polystyrene microtiter plates were purchased from Corning, Inc. (USA). The size and morphology of Ab-GQD and Ag-SQD were characterized using a JEOL-2100F transmission electron microscopy (TEM) equipped with an Oxford Instrument EDS system operating at 200 kV. The Tecan multifunctional fluorescence microplate reader (Austria) was used to measure fluorescent signals changes of the probe.

The GQD was prepared by the hydrothermal method making use of the graphene oxide as precursor [27–28]. The graphene oxide was synthesized using the modified Hummers method. The 2 mg graphene oxide was placed into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to about 180 ℃ for 5 h. The suspension was ultrasonicated and centrifugation for 1 h to obtain GQD solution. The SQD (CdTe QD) were synthesized according to previous reports [29]. In brief, NaHTe solution was synthesized by the reaction of NaBH4 with tellurium at a 2:1 M ratio. Afterward, the obtained solution was mixed with 1.25 mM CdCl2 aqueous solution and 3-mercaptopropyl acid. After refluxed for 9 h, the stable SQD was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the dual-QDs ratios fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: The insulin Ag and monoclonal against insulin Ab were associated SQD and GQD respectively by previous methods with some modifications [30–31]. Concretely, 50 μL 5 mg mL−1 EDC was added into 100 μL SQD solution. The mixture was shaken on a vortex mixer for 2 min and bath sonicated for 15 min. After that, 50 μL 5 mg mL-1Ag was added into the mixture and incubated for 40 min at room temperature (RT). On the other hand, 40 μL 1 mg mL−1 GQD, 1 mL 1 mg mL-1Ab and 57 μL 10 mg mL−1 EDC were mixed and incubated for 2 h at RT under continuous shaking in the dark. All conjugate solution was filtered by a PES membrane unit to remove the excess reactants to obtain Ag-SQD and Ab-GQD conjugates respectively. Then, the dual-QDs ratios fluorescent probe would be formed by mixing Ab- GQD and Ag-SQD. The special fluorescent features of the probe were systematically investigated by changing its composition ratio and studied its cause of formation.

The fluorescent characteristics of the dual-QDs ratios fluorescent probe were monitored by fluorescence microplate reader. The fluorescent excitation-emission mappings of Ab-GQD were obtained from the emission data of GQD (ranging from 400 nm to 530 nm) with the use of different excitation wavelengths (ranging from 350 nm to 400 nm). The FRET was monitored by comparing fluorescent spectra of Ab-GQD at different concentration of Ag-SQD (from 0.0125 to 0.8 µM). The efficiency of FRET has been calculated from the following equation:
(1)
E = 1 - (
F
DA
/
F D
)
where FDA and FD are the fluorescent intensity of the donor in the prescence and absence of the acceptor respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Insulin detection of the dual-QDs ratios fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: In order to avoid the interference of stray light, the probe was placed in the black polystyrene microtiter plates. After optimization of the detection conditions (excitation wavelength, reaction pH, temperature and composition ratio), the probe was incubated with different target insulin concentration (0 to 250 ng mL−1) for 5 min at 37℃ and the dual fluorescent intensity changes at 450 nm and 625 nm of the probe was monitored under excitation lights at 360 nm respectively. For observing the selectivity and stability, the prepared biological interfering ions (Fe2+, Cu2+, Na+ and NH4+), analogs of insulin (CRP, urea and BSA) and the sodium salt with different concentration were immersed into the probe one after another and the ratio fluorescence changes were recorded with the fluorescence microplate reader.

The biosensing experiments of this study were repeated using the human serum for estimating practical application of the dual-QDs ratios fluorescent probe in real sample condition. For this purpose, the blood samples were centrifuged at 2000 rpm for 15 min to obtain serum. Three different concentrations of stranded insulin were subsequently spiked into the serum samples, and stored at -20 ℃ until analysis by the probe and traditional ELISA method.

As it can be seen in Fig. 2 a and Fig. 2b, Ab-GQD and Ag-SQD had typical spherical morphology with the diameter of about 10 nm and 5 nm respectively yielded the large specific surface area. After mixing them, Ab-GQD and Ag-SQD can be distributed independently (Fig. 2c), which was favorable to free entry and specific recognition of target molecules. Under UV illumination, Ab-GQD and Ag-SQD exhibited intense blue and red fluorescence, respectively (inset of Fig. 2a and Fig. 2b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual fluorescent characteristics of the probe
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in the fluorescent excitation-emission mappings (Fig. 3 a, Fig. 3b and Fig. 3c), the fluorescent emission of Ab-GQD component was enhanced with an increase of concentration, C0 (∼0.0128 mg mL−1), C0/4 and C0/8. This higher fluorescent intensity of Ab-GQD in higher concentration range was likely consistent with the reported AIE effect [32]. It was reported that AIE is exactly opposite to the general aggregation-induced quenching (ACQ) effect. AIE active fluorogens are weak-emissive in low concentration solution but become highly fluorescence when increased concentration because the restriction of the intramolecular rotations (RIR) due to the physical constraint in the aggregates opens radiative decay channel [23,33]. Although some studies describe the AIE phenomenon of GQDs, they have different manifestations [32,34,35]. Compared with the reported GQDs, our material showed a narrower AIE activity range, which would be related to the structure, surface morphology and solvent environment of GQDs [34]. In addition, the emission peaks were red-shifted with an increase of excitation wavelength in Fig. 3a-3c.

As can be seen in Fig. 4 a, there was the spectral overlap between the Ab- GQD emission spectrum and the Ag-SQD excitation spectra, which providing the key condition for FRET occurrence. In Fig. 4b, it was indicated that there have been FRET happened because the fluorescent intensity of Ab-GQD was decreased and that of Ag-SQD was increased at the same time. Among them, Ab-GQD and Ag-SQD were worked as the energy donor and acceptor respectively although they are usually as the energy donor in other many FRET formats [36]. Similar phenomenon was also described in the related report [37]. It was learned that the effective donor usually emitted bluer and typically exhibited higher quantum yield than the acceptor as was necessary to efficient energy storage to emit the enough energy. In this study, Ab-GQD had the bluer emission (450 nm) and higher quantum yield (6.7%) than the Ag-SQD’s (625 nm and 3.9 %) as shown in Table S1. Hence, Ab-GQD met the above characteristics and can be worked as energy donor instead of Ag-SQD. For a 0.0125 µM addition of Ag-SQD to the Ab-GQD solution only a 2% energy transfer occurred, whereas for 0.82 µM addition the energy transfer was 41, as shown in Fig. 4c. This demonstrated that the efficiency of the energy transfer increased with the gradual addition of Ag-SQD. This phenomenon may be due to the fact that more acceptor can widen the available FRET pathways and make the probability of de-excitation via FRET become greater [37].

As shown in Fig. 4b, the probe exhibited a maximum fluorescent signal at 450 nm (Ab-GQD) and 625 nm (Ag-SQD) respectively. The fluorescent intensity of Ab-GQD was improved based on AIE, however it was then decreased, and the fluorescent intensity of Ag-SQD was increased through FRET, thus creating the special dual fluorescent lights. In the probe, the fluorescent emission bandwidth of Ab-GQD and Ag-SQD was narrow and symmetric with a full width at a half-maximum of about 58 nm and 43 nm respectively. In addition, Ab-GQD and Ag-SQD can be excited with short-wavelength light sources and their emission peaks had a longer distance (450 nm and 625 nm). In this case, the disturbing background signal was significantly avoided. All above results indicated that the formed the probe had the stable dual fluorescent lights. Through the immune competition reactions, the target molecules would destroy the special probe state and change the special dual fluorescence for the purpose of ratio fluorescent detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The dual-QDs ratios fluorescent probe for insulin detection
ce:section.ce:para: After optimization, 1:4 as Ab-GQD and Ag-SQD ratio, pH 7.2 0.01 M PBS buffer as basic reaction solution, 37 ℃ as incubation temperature and 360 nm as excitation wavelength were chosen in the following experiment (As shown in supplement information).

Under optimal conditions, the dual-QDs ratios fluorescent probe was used to detect insulin. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the fluorescent intensity at 450 nm increased and that at 625 nm decreased with increase of insulin concentration. However, different from sensing behavior of the dual-QDs ratios fluorescent probe, there were few fluorescent intensity changes of Ab-GQD and Ag-SQD when adding insulin to Ab-GQD and Ag-SQD separately in Fig. 5b. It was indicated that the independent use of Ab-GQD and Ag-SQD cannot detect target insulin. Therefore, the detection behavior of the dual-QDs ratios fluorescent probe would be resulted from FRET interaction of Ab-GQD with Ag-SQD. The FRET interaction gave rise to the special dual fluorescent lights of the probe when without target insulin (as described in Fig. 4b). With an increase addition of insulin, more and more insulin replaced the Ag-SQD of probe and intervened in the interaction, thus causing increased fluorescent intensity of Ab-GQD (450 nm) and decreased that of Ag-SQD (625 nm).

A good linear relationship between the data of F450/F625 and the insulin concentration was obtained in the range from 0.032 to 250 ng mL−1 in Fig. 5c. The corresponding regression coefficient was 0.998, and the limit of detection (LOD) for insulin was 0.045 ng mL−1. The higher sensitivity would be resulted from decreased systematic errors of the ratio signals detection because the most of methods for insulin were based on a single detection signal, which cannot avoid the background interference, finally affecting the detection sensitivity.

The response characteristics of the probe were investigated by immersing insulin solution (2 ng mL−1) for 1, 3, 5, 10, 20, 30 and 40 min. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the probe exhibited rapid response to insulin and reached adsorptive equilibrium within 5 min. This was because that our proposed approach need not sophisticated detection equipment and a large number of separate analytical procedures, thus showing many advantages, such as low cost, easy construction and simple operation.

The selectivity and biological stability of the biosensor was investigated with insulin analogs (CRP, urea and BSA) as the references and the biological interfering ions (Fe2+, Cu2+, Na+ and NH4+) in Fig. 6b. Apart from obvious F450/F625 changes of the probe to insulin, there was a low response to the insulin analogs and biological interfering ions. In addition, stability of the probe was tested under the different sodium concentration (from 120 to 160 mmol L-1). As shown in Fig. 6c, there was a low response to sodium with different concentration. It was indicated that the probe possessed great selectivity and certain stability for insulin detection.

The probe was used to analyze insulin in human serum to further verify the applicability in a biological matrix. In Table 1 , the recoveries were ranged from 96.3% to 107.5% while all RSDs of the insulin were less than 5%. In addition, determination values of insulin were in accordance with those in clinical routine ELISA testing kit. But the values of RSDs were lower than the testing kit. Therefore, the results suggested the probe performed higher stability and feasibility and could be applied for the determination of insulin in real samples.

In summary, the novel sensing probe was successfully prepared for the ratio fluorescent detection of insulin. After optimization, the probe was demonstrated to be simple, sensitive, stable and selective for detecting insulin. The present work may open a pathway for a facile and fast method to detect other biomolecules. Further research should be focused on the systemically studying the AIE phenomenon of GQDs and exploring physical and chemical characteristics of the probe for improving sensitivity and stability to facilitate its application in other biological real samples testing.

Guanggui Yu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft. Zhong Sun: Formal analysis, Investigation. Yuntang Wu: Supervision, Project administration. Na Sai: Resources, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the funding of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant 81302430) and Natural Science Foundation of Tianjin (Grant 18JCQNJC10800), and Chinese Nutrition Society-Bright Moon Seaweed Nutrition and Health Research Fund (Grant CNS-BMSG2020A60).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.120641.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
